output.final_deliverable:
Missing evidence: No search/visit results were provided in `merged`, so I don’t have any sources to cite for chemistry, history, health, or product examples. To comply with the requirement to use only evidence from searches/visits and include inline URL citations, I need web search + page visits (and then I can synthesize across domains).

What I will produce once sources are available (outline of the deliverable):
1) **PTFE chemical structure & properties**
   - Repeat unit, bonding, why it’s chemically inert and thermally stable.
2) **Historical development**
   - Discovery, commercial adoption, and expansion into cookware and industrial uses.
3) **Health controversies**
   - Distinguish PTFE polymer vs. processing aids (e.g., legacy PFOA), and also thermal decomposition/overheating concerns.
   - Summarize major regulatory/industry phase-outs and key toxicology findings.
4) **Current products using PTFE**
   - Cookware coatings, industrial linings, gaskets/seals, wire/cable insulation, medical devices, etc., with concrete product/manufacturer examples.
5) **Synthesis: chemistry → health concerns → modern mitigations**
   - Explain how the C–F backbone and high inertness relate to low reactivity in normal use, while high-heat decomposition can generate fumes/particles.
   - Explain how historical use of certain fluorinated processing aids created separate contamination issues, and how modern manufacturing/product standards (e.g., PFOA-free claims, temperature-use guidance, improved coatings) address these.

If you want, I can proceed to run searches and visit sources now; once I’ve collected evidence, I’ll return the full, cited synthesis.

results.title: Polytetrafluoroethylene - Wikipedia
results.description: DuPont's spin-off Chemours currently manufactures PTFE using an alternative chemical it calls GenX, another PFAS. Although GenX was designed to be less persistent in the environment compared to PFOA, its effects may be equally harmful or even more detrimental than those of the chemical it has replaced. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) was accidentally discovered in 1938 by Roy J. Plunkett while he was working in Chemours Chambers Works plant in New Jersey for DuPont.

results.title: Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) | Chemistry | Research Starters | EBSCO Research
results.description: The accidental discovery of PTFE came in 1938 when Dr. Roy Plunkett, an American chemist working in DuPont's laboratories, was attempting to find a new refrigeration coolant. During his experiments, Plunkett inadvertently froze a sample of tetrafluoroethylene, a colorless, odorless, flammable, and slightly toxic gas. He discovered that his error caused the gas to turn into a powder that was polymerized—it had undergone a chemical change that resulted in long chains of connected molecules instead of individual molecules.

results.title: PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene) - Uses, Structure, & Properties
results.description: PTFE stands for Polytetrafluoroethylene or PTFE, the commonly used versatile, high-performance fluoropolymer. PTFE was first discovered 'accidentally' in 1938 by Dr. Plunkett at DuPont. After that PTFE was made commercially available in 1947 with the trademark 'Teflon™' from Chemours. It was the discovery of PTFE that accelerated the development of the other fluoropolymers. PTFE is made up of carbon and fluorine atoms. The chemical structure of PTFE [CF2-CF2]n is like that of polyethylene (PE).

results.title: PTFE (Teflon): Properties, Applications, & Uses | DoonX
results.description: Roy Plunkett's 1938 "failure" at DuPont became one of chemistry's greatest accidents - while experimenting with refrigerants, he unexpectedly synthesized a white powder that marked the birth of the world's first fluoropolymer. PTFE [CF2-CF2]n has a chemical structure similar to polyethylene (PE), with the key difference being that all hydrogen atoms in PE are completely replaced by fluorine.

results.title: Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) | Definition, Structure, Uses, & Facts | Britannica
results.description: The polymer consists of a chain of carbon atoms with two fluorine atoms bonded to each carbon: ... The fluorine atoms surround the carbon chain like a protective sheath, creating a chemically inert and relatively dense molecule with very strong carbon-fluorine bonds.

results.title: What Is PTFE(Polytetrafluoroethylene)? - Aokai PTFE
results.description: Polytetrafluoroethylene is a linear polymer composed of carbon (C) and fluorine (F) atoms, with the chemical formula (C2F4)n, where n is the number of monomer units. The structure of PTFE can be expressed as: -CF2-CF2-CF2-CF2-

results.title: What is Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE): Chemical Structure, Properties, Applications – PAKTECHPOINT
results.description: PTFE is a white, waxy solid that is inert to most chemicals, making it an ideal choice for various applications where resistance to corrosion and high temperatures is required. PTFE was discovered in 1938 by a chemist named Roy Plunkett while working for the chemical company DuPont.

results.title: Poly(tetrafluoroethylene) | 9002-84-0
results.description: PTFE;POLYTETRAFLUOROETHYLENE;TEFLON;PTFE poly(tetrafluoroethylene);Polyflon;PTFE POWDER;Fluoropolymer;Politef;Fluoroflex;Fluorolene ... Poly-(Tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE, Brand name: Teflon, Fluon, Hostaflon, and Polyflon) is a kind of strong, tough, waxy and nonflammable synthetic resin. Its structure formula is [CF2-CF2]n with a carbon backbone chain. It is produced through the free-radical polymerization of tetrafluoroethylene.

results.title: The Story of PTFE - Features - The Chemical Engineer
results.description: It was obvious to Plunkett that it was the polymer polytetrafluorethylene (PTFE), despite chemists having decided TFE could not polymerise. Its main characteristic was inertness. He finally determined the mechanism and patented it: catalysis by some metal salts under pressure over several days giving varying products of white or brown powder or a jelly. However, he had nothing further to do with the product, as DuPont put him in charge of tetraethyl lead and later Freon production.

results.title: The Fascinating History of PTFE: From Accidental Discovery to Industrial Marvel - jinshiling ptfe manufacturer
results.description: This unexpected substance was polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), a polymer formed when TFE gas spontaneously polymerized under high pressure. Upon further investigation, Plunkett discovered that PTFE had several remarkable characteristics: Extreme Non-Stick Properties: PTFE exhibited an incredibly low coefficient of friction, making it unlike any material known at the time. Chemical Resistance: It was highly resistant to acids, bases, and solvents, making it chemically inert.
